h1Title: Anal Fistula: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: An anal fistula is an abnormal tunnel connecting the anal canal or rectum to the skin surrounding the anus, almost always arising after a perianal abscess that failed to drain fully or heal. The condition affects roughly 1-2 per 10,000 people per year, with men affected two to three times more often than women. The classic presentation is a persistent discharge of pus, blood, or stool from a small opening near the anus, often with recurrent local swelling and discomfort. Without surgery, spontaneous healing is rare and recurrent abscess formation is the rule. Definitive treatment is surgical; fistulotomy cures 85-95% of simple tracts, while complex fistulas — those traversing the external sphincter — require sphincter-sparing techniques to prevent incontinence.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence approximately 1.2-2.8 per 10,000 population in Europe (colorectal literature, 2010s); higher in inflammatory bowel disease cohorts where perianal fistulas affect 15-35% of Crohn's disease patients
keyStats.demographics: Men 2-3× more affected than women; peak incidence age 30-50 years; Crohn's disease is a major risk factor in younger patients
keyStats.avgAge: Cryptoglandular fistulas peak in the third to fifth decades; Crohn's-related fistulas often appear in the second to fourth decades
keyStats.globalCases: No precise global prevalence; Crohn's disease-associated perianal fistulas affect an estimated 2-3 million patients worldwide

definition: Anal fistula (ICD-10: K60.3) is a chronic inflammatory tract lined by granulation tissue that communicates between the anorectum and the perianal skin. In the vast majority of cases — over 90% — the internal opening lies at the dentate line within the anal crypt, corresponding to the origin of a crypt gland infection (the cryptoglandular theory). The tract may then travel through the sphincter complex in four classic patterns first described by Parks: intersphincteric (45%), transsphincteric (30%), suprasphincteric (20%), and extrasphincteric (5%). A separate classification — the American Society of Colon and Rectal Surgeons (ASCRS) simple versus complex scheme — is used to guide therapy: simple fistulas cross less than 30% of the external sphincter and have no associated complicating features; complex fistulas cross more muscle, are high, are associated with Crohn's disease or prior radiation, or were recurrent. Crohn's disease accounts for up to 25% of cases in colorectal referral practices, and fistula-in-ano in this context is biologically distinct — driven by transmural inflammation rather than crypt gland infection — and requires combined medical and surgical management. The condition is managed by colorectal surgeons, with gastroenterologists comanaging Crohn's-related disease.

typesClassification.description: The most common type (45%). The tract passes down the intersphincteric space to the perianal skin without crossing the external sphincter. Low risk of incontinence after fistulotomy.

About 30% of cases. The tract crosses through the external sphincter at varying heights. Higher tracts carry significant incontinence risk with simple fistulotomy; sphincter-sparing techniques are preferred.
Roughly 20% of cases. Tract passes above the puborectalis through the levator ani, then down between the external sphincter and levator. Complex management required.
Least common, approximately 5%. The tract bypasses both sphincters entirely, opening above the levator. Usually secondary to trauma, surgery, Crohn's disease, or pelvic infection.

Multiple tracts, bilateral, or recurring fistulas in a patient with Crohn's disease. Treated with combined immunosuppression (biologic agents) and drainage/seton procedures; permanent stoma sometimes required in refractory cases.

primarySymptoms: Persistent or intermittent discharge of pus, blood, or fecal matter from a small external opening near the anus, often staining underclothes and requiring a pad.
Perianal pain or throbbing discomfort that worsens on sitting, walking, or defecation, and temporarily improves when discharge occurs and pressure is relieved.
Swelling and redness around the anal opening, often recurrent, representing reaccumulation of pus when the fistula tract blocks intermittently.
Pruritus ani (intense anal itching) caused by chronic moisture and bacterial contamination from the discharge on perianal skin.
Systemic fever and malaise during acute abscess reformation when the tract becomes obstructed and pus cannot drain freely.
A palpable firm cord or indurated tract beneath the perianal skin, detected by the patient or on physical examination, corresponding to the fistula track.
Recurrent perianal abscess at the same anatomical location, a hallmark of underlying fistula that was not identified at initial drainage.
Anal irritation with excoriated or macerated perianal skin from prolonged contact with purulent discharge.
Occasional bleeding with bowel movements when the internal opening in the anal canal is irritated by passing stool.
Difficulty sitting comfortably for extended periods due to persistent perianal discomfort and pressure on the diseased tissue.
earlyWarningSigns: A painful, tense perianal swelling that resolves spontaneously or after drainage but recurs within weeks to months at the same spot — classic sequence of abscess-fistula formation.
Persistent small amount of discharge from the perianal skin more than four weeks after surgical drainage of a perianal abscess.
A pit or dimple visible on perianal inspection at the location of a previous abscess, indicating the external opening of a developing fistula.
New perianal pain in a patient with Crohn's disease, even without visible swelling, as Crohn's fistulas may be deeply placed before becoming clinically apparent.
emergencySigns: Rapidly spreading perianal, perineal, or scrotal redness, induration, and crepitus with systemic toxicity — this presentation requires urgent surgical assessment to exclude Fournier's gangrene, a necrotizing fasciitis with mortality up to 20-40%.
High fever above 38.5°C with inability to sit and marked perianal swelling — acute abscess under tension requiring same-day incision and drainage to prevent septic extension.
New bowel incontinence after a previous sphincter operation — suggests unexpected sphincter injury and warrants urgent colorectal review.
Perianal pain with lower limb swelling, erythema tracking up the buttock, or signs of systemic sepsis — indicates extension of infection along fascial planes into the ischiorectal space or pelvis.

causes.cause: Perianal abscess from infected anal glands (cryptoglandular origin)
causes.description: The anal canal contains 6-10 mucus-secreting cryptoglandular glands at the dentate line. Obstruction of a gland duct leads to abscess formation in the intersphincteric space. When the abscess ruptures or is drained without identifying the internal opening, a persistent tract lined with granulation tissue remains — the fistula.

Transmural bowel inflammation in Crohn's disease promotes fistula formation through full-thickness intestinal wall injury. Perianal fistulas develop in 15-35% of Crohn's patients, often before intestinal symptoms are diagnosed. These fistulas tend to be complex, bilateral, high-lying, and resistant to conventional surgical excision.

Anal instrumentation, obstetric tears (particularly third- and fourth-degree perineal lacerations), penetrating injuries, and impacted foreign bodies can create false passages that epithelialize into persistent fistulas. These typically track in unusual planes outside the Parks classification.

Fistulotomy, hemorrhoidectomy, and lateral internal sphincterotomy can occasionally result in a secondary fistula if wound healing is complicated by infection or if a rectal flap procedure breaks down. Anastomotic failure after proctectomy or rectal resection creates a different variant — the rectovaginal or rectoperineal fistula.

Pelvic inflammatory disease, diverticular abscess, appendiceal or tubo-ovarian abscess rupturing into the rectum, or prior pelvic radiation therapy can all create extrasphincteric fistulas by direct erosion of rectal or perineal tissues.

Anorectal tuberculosis accounts for 1-5% of fistulas in high-prevalence settings, characteristically producing multiple external openings with pale, overhanging skin edges. Actinomycosis, lymphogranuloma venereum, and hidradenitis suppurativa of the perianal region produce fistula-like tracts in their advanced stages.

riskFactors.description: Men are 2-3× more likely to develop anal fistulas than women, probably due to higher androgenic activity in cryptoglandular glands and differing anorectal anatomy.

Cryptoglandular anal fistulas peak in the active, working-age population. Pediatric fistulas are common in male infants and usually resolve; elderly fistulas are more likely to have underlying cancer or radiation as the cause.
Patients with Crohn's disease have a 15-35% lifetime risk of perianal fistula. Risk correlates with colonic disease involvement; ileal-only Crohn's carries a lower risk.

Impaired neutrophil function and microvascular disease in poorly controlled diabetes increase the risk of perianal abscess formation and impair healing after surgical treatment, raising recurrence rates.
HIV infection, organ-transplant immunosuppression, and high-dose corticosteroid therapy reduce the ability to clear perianal infection, increasing the risk of abscess extension and fistula formation.

riskFactors.factor: Prior perianal abscess without fistula identification
riskFactors.description: Roughly 30-50% of perianal abscesses have an associated fistula. Failure to identify and address the internal opening at drainage leaves a chronic tract that recurs as abscess or discharging fistula.

Prior pelvic radiotherapy for prostate, cervical, rectal, or bladder cancer damages rectal mucosa and submucosa, creating a chronic fibrotic tissue environment where fistulas may form years after treatment.

affectedDemographics: Men aged 30-50 years account for the majority of cryptoglandular anal fistulas; the male-to-female ratio is approximately 2-3:1.
Crohn's disease patients: up to 35% develop perianal fistulas; risk is highest with Crohn's colitis versus pure ileal disease.
Infants (predominantly male) can develop simple intersphincteric fistulas in the first year of life, often resolving spontaneously without surgery.
Patients with diabetes mellitus or HIV infection are over-represented in perianal sepsis series because of impaired local immunity.
diagnosisOverview: Clinical diagnosis of anal fistula begins with history and perineal inspection. The classic finding is one or more external openings — small pink or red granulating holes — visible near the anus, often with surrounding scarring, from which pus can be expressed. Goodsall's rule provides a useful anatomical guide: external openings posterior to a transverse anal line generally have a curved tract leading to the posterior midline; anterior openings typically track directly to the nearest crypt. Digital rectal examination detects the firm submucosal cord of the tract and may identify the internal opening as a nodular pit at the dentate line. Proctoscopy or anoscopy confirms the internal opening and excludes rectal disease. Imaging is essential before surgery for complex fistulas. MRI of the pelvis with endoanal coil or phased-array coil is the gold standard: it delineates tract anatomy, identifies secondary extensions and horseshoe tracts, and determines the sphincter level crossed with sensitivity above 85% and specificity above 90% in published series. Endoanal ultrasound — particularly three-dimensional hydrogen peroxide-enhanced ultrasound — offers comparable accuracy for simple fistulas. Examination under anesthesia (EUA) performed by an experienced colorectal surgeon remains the definitive diagnostic and planning step in complex or recurrent cases. In suspected Crohn's-related fistulas, colonoscopy and small-bowel imaging confirm the diagnosis, and fistula recurrence rate correlates with luminal disease activity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies external opening(s), palpates the fistula tract, and locates the internal opening at the dentate line
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient examination on a procedure table. The clinician inspects the perineum, inserts a small anoscope with a light, and may use a fine probe or hydrogen peroxide injection to confirm the tract. Mild discomfort for a few minutes; no sedation required in most cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging for complex and recurrent fistulas; maps tract anatomy relative to the sphincter complex and identifies secondary extensions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in an MRI scanner. No radiation. Patients lie still; sedation is rarely needed. A gel may be placed into the rectum before endoanal coil imaging. Results reviewed by a specialist radiologist within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Endoanal ultrasound (EAUS) with hydrogen peroxide enhancement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Delineates the fistula tract and sphincter anatomy; especially useful for simple intersphincteric and transsphincteric fistulas
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small ultrasound probe is inserted into the anal canal — similar to a digital rectal examination. Takes about 10-15 minutes in an outpatient or procedure room setting.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnostic and operative planning step: allows gentle probing of the tract, fistulogram injection, and direct sphincter assessment when office examination is limited by pain or anatomy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in the operating theatre under general or spinal anesthesia. Usually combined with the surgical procedure itself if indicated. Day-case or short-stay admission.

diagnosticTests.test: Colonoscopy and small-bowel MRI or CT enterography
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms or excludes Crohn's disease in patients with complex, recurrent, multiple, or unusual fistulas
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Colonoscopy requires bowel preparation the day before. CT or MRI enterography uses oral contrast and is performed in radiology; results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Delineates complex or unusual tracts when MRI is unavailable or contraindicated; less commonly used in the MRI era but still performed for extrasphincteric or rectovaginal fistulas
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Water-soluble contrast is injected through the external opening under X-ray guidance. Outpatient procedure taking 20-30 minutes; mild local pressure during injection.

treatmentOverview: Treatment selection depends on fistula classification (simple versus complex), sphincter involvement, presence of Crohn's disease, prior surgery, and continence status. For simple low fistulas (intersphincteric and low transsphincteric), fistulotomy — laying open the tract — achieves cure rates of 85-95% with minimal incontinence risk when less than 30% of the external sphincter is divided. The wound heals by secondary intention over 4-12 weeks. For complex fistulas that cross significant sphincter muscle, sphincter-sparing techniques are used. A cutting seton (a thread progressively tightened to divide the muscle slowly) is largely being replaced by loose (draining) seton placement followed by a definitive sphincter-sparing repair. Mucosal advancement flap — a partial-thickness rectal flap rotated to cover the internal opening — has a success rate of 50-75% at three years. The LIFT (ligation of intersphincteric fistula tract) procedure, described in 2007, has success rates of 60-80% with no sphincter division. Anal fistula plug (bioprosthetic plug) and fibrin glue injection have lower and less durable cure rates (30-50%) but are appropriate for patients with pre-existing sphincter compromise. For Crohn's-associated fistulas, the ASCRS 2021 and ACG 2018 guidelines recommend combined biologics and surgery: anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab) achieve fistula closure in approximately 35-50% of patients on placebo-controlled trials, and ustekinumab is an emerging option. Loose seton drainage alongside biologic therapy reduces septic complications and improves biologic response rates. Stem cell therapy (darvadstrocel, an allogeneic adipose-derived stem cell product) is approved in the European Union for Crohn's perianal fistula and achieves combined remission (clinical and radiological) in 56% versus 39% on placebo at 52 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Infliximab (5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6 then q8w)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-TNF biologic used in Crohn's-associated fistulas. Reduces fistula drainage and achieves closure when combined with surgical seton drainage. Requires tuberculosis and hepatitis B screening before initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fistula closure in approximately 36-55% of patients in randomized trials (ACCENT II trial PMID 12767095); maintenance therapy sustains response better than induction alone

medicalTreatments.name: Adalimumab (160/80/40 mg SC induction then 40 mg q2w)
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous anti-TNF alternative to infliximab for Crohn's fistulas. Preferable for self-administration and when infliximab antibodies have developed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fistula closure in approximately 33% versus 13% on placebo at 26 weeks (CHARM trial)

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics (ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily, metronidazole 400 mg three times daily for 6-12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line adjunctive treatment for Crohn's perianal fistula and for controlling sepsis around cryptoglandular fistulas before surgery. Not curative as monotherapy but reduce drainage and inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fistula response (not complete closure) in approximately 40-50% of Crohn's patients in prospective series; relapse nearly universal on stopping antibiotics

medicalTreatments.name: Darvadstrocel (allogeneic expanded adipose-derived stem cells, 120 million cells intralesional injection)
medicalTreatments.description: EU-approved cell therapy for complex Crohn's perianal fistulas refractory to at least one conventional or biologic therapy. Injected into the fistula wall and surrounding tissue after surgical tract debridement and closure of the internal opening.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined remission (clinical + MRI closure) 56% versus 39% on placebo at 52 weeks (ADMIRE-CD trial PMID 27914428)

medicalTreatments.name: Fibrin glue injection into the fistula tract
medicalTreatments.description: Biological sealant injected to occlude the tract after curettage. Minimally invasive, preserves sphincter. Useful in patients with poor sphincter reserve or as a bridge in Crohn's disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Initial closure rates 50-60%; long-term sustained closure reported in only 15-30% of cases

surgicalOptions.name: Fistulotomy (laying open)
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive procedure for simple low intersphincteric and low transsphincteric fistulas. The tract is probed, laid open with electrocautery or scalpel, curetted, and left to heal by secondary intention over 4-12 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure rate 85-95% for simple low fistulas; incontinence risk under 5% when less than 30% of external sphincter is divided

surgicalOptions.name: LIFT procedure (ligation of intersphincteric fistula tract)
surgicalOptions.description: The intersphincteric portion of the tract is identified, ligated, and divided through an intersphincteric groove incision. No sphincter muscle is cut. Appropriate for transsphincteric fistulas crossing the external sphincter.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Primary healing rate 60-80% in systematic reviews; continence almost always preserved; recurrences are usually simple intersphincteric tracts amenable to repeat procedures

surgicalOptions.name: Mucosal advancement flap repair
surgicalOptions.description: A partial-thickness rectal mucosal and submucosal flap is raised, the internal opening debrided, and the flap advanced to cover it without tension. Maintains sphincter integrity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rates 50-75% at two to three years; prior irradiation, active Crohn's disease, and smoking reduce success

surgicalOptions.name: Loose seton insertion
surgicalOptions.description: A non-cutting thread passed through the tract and tied loosely. Drains the tract, prevents abscess recurrence, and allows fibrosis before a definitive sphincter-sparing repair. Frequently used as a two-stage procedure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Not curative alone; used to control sepsis and condition the tract before fistulotomy or sphincter-sparing repair. Associated with 90-95% sepsis control rates.

surgicalOptions.name: Anal fistula plug (bioprosthetic plug)
surgicalOptions.description: A cone-shaped bioresorbable plug (porcine small intestinal submucosa) is inserted to obliterate the tract and stimulate tissue ingrowth. Preserves sphincter muscle completely.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial trials reported 80% success but subsequent larger series show 20-50% long-term closure; most useful in Crohn's-associated fistulas or patients with compromised sphincters

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical tacrolimus (0.1% ointment to internal opening)
alternativeTreatments.description: Small case series in Crohn's fistulas suggest partial response; not approved as monotherapy but may reduce local inflammation during biologic therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: Anal fistulas should be evaluated by a colorectal surgeon, as even apparently simple tracts can involve the sphincter in ways not apparent on examination. Crohn's-associated fistulas require joint management by a gastroenterologist for biologic therapy and a colorectal surgeon for drainage and surgical intervention.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a colorectal surgeon with specific experience in anorectal disease and, for complex or Crohn's-related fistulas, one working in an IBD multidisciplinary team. High-volume centers performing LIFT, advancement flap, and darvadstrocel procedures offer the best outcomes for recurrent or sphincter-threatening disease.
hospitalCriteria: Colorectal surgery unit with anorectal physiology testing capability
MRI pelvis service with subspecialized GI radiology reporting
IBD multidisciplinary team for Crohn's-related fistulas
Access to biologic therapies (anti-TNF agents) and darvadstrocel
Endoanal ultrasound in the surgical outpatient setting
keyFacilities: Colorectal surgery outpatient clinics with anorectal procedure rooms
Tertiary IBD centres with combined gastroenterology-surgical teams
Day-surgery units for EUA and seton procedures
preventionStrategies: Ensure complete drainage and identification of the internal fistula opening at the time of initial perianal abscess surgery — the most important preventable cause of chronic fistula is inadequate primary drainage.
Maintain glycemic control in diabetes mellitus to reduce perianal infection risk and improve post-surgical wound healing.
Achieve sustained remission in Crohn's disease with effective biologic therapy to reduce the risk of fistula formation and recurrence after repair.
Avoid anal trauma from instrumentation or foreign bodies in the anorectal canal.
Seek prompt medical attention for any perianal swelling, pain, or discharge rather than allowing abscess to rupture spontaneously, which increases the risk of complex tract formation.
lifestyleModifications: Keep the perianal area clean and dry; gentle washing after each bowel movement, pat dry rather than rubbing.
Wear breathable cotton underwear and use absorbent pads to manage discharge while awaiting or recovering from surgery.
Avoid prolonged sitting on hard surfaces during the active discharge phase; use a donut cushion or padded seat.
Maintain a high-fiber diet (25-30 g/day) and adequate hydration to produce soft, formed stools that minimize local trauma during defecation.
Abstain from smoking, which impairs wound healing and increases the risk of fistula recurrence after repair.
Attend all follow-up appointments; recurrence after surgery should be investigated early rather than left to progress to a new abscess.

recommended: High-fiber foods (whole grains, vegetables, legumes, fruit) to produce soft stools and reduce straining
Adequate daily fluid intake (1.5-2 L) to avoid constipation
Protein-rich foods (lean meat, eggs, legumes) to support wound healing after surgery
Probiotic-containing foods or supplements where IBD-related fistula is present, though evidence is limited
avoid: Low-fiber, highly refined foods that promote hard stools and straining
Excessive alcohol, which impairs wound healing and immunity
Spicy or very fatty foods that increase stool frequency and perianal irritation in inflamed disease
Food-triggering Crohn's flares specific to the individual patient, identified through dietary diary

exerciseGuidelines: Light walking and gentle exercise are encouraged within a week after fistulotomy or seton placement. Strenuous activities, cycling, and swimming should be avoided for four to six weeks after surgery. After wound healing, full activity including sport is unrestricted.
dailyManagement: Change perianal pads or dressings as needed; note amount and character of discharge and report significant changes to the surgical team.
Perform sitz baths (warm water soaks for 10-15 minutes) two to three times daily, especially after bowel movements, to relieve discomfort and keep the wound clean.
Take prescribed stool softeners or bulking agents as directed to prevent constipation and avoid straining.
Complete the full course of antibiotics if prescribed for abscess control before surgery.
For Crohn's patients, take biologic injections or infusions as scheduled and report any new perianal swelling or increased pain immediately.
Attend wound-check appointments at one to two weeks after surgery and again at six to eight weeks; a community nurse or specialist nurse can assist with dressing changes.
prognosis: For simple cryptoglandular fistulas treated by fistulotomy, the prognosis is excellent. Cure rates of 85-95% are reported and minor incontinence (to flatus or liquid) occurs in fewer than 10% of patients. Wound healing is typically complete in four to twelve weeks. Complex fistulas crossing significant sphincter muscle have lower cure rates of 50-80% depending on the technique, and up to 30% may require repeat surgery. Crohn's-associated fistulas have the most guarded prognosis: biologic therapy achieves sustained closure in only 35-55% at one year, and long-term relapse rates exceed 50% in trials extending beyond three years. Fistulas that do not respond to combined medical and surgical management may require permanent defunctioning stoma or proctectomy in approximately 10-15% of Crohn's cases. Fistula-related incontinence significantly impairs quality of life; anorectal physiology testing should guide repair choice, particularly in women with obstetric sphincter damage or patients with prior anorectal surgery.
recoveryTimeline: After fistulotomy, most wounds heal within four to eight weeks for short simple tracts and eight to twelve weeks for larger wounds. Seton procedures require three to twelve months before a definitive repair is performed. After LIFT or advancement flap, patients return to normal activity within two to four weeks but require follow-up imaging at three months to confirm closure.
complications: Fecal incontinence — partial or complete — resulting from sphincter division during surgery; risk increases with the height and extent of muscle divided and with pre-existing sphincter damage from obstetric injury or prior surgery.
Fistula recurrence after surgery, occurring in 10-30% of complex cases; recurrence usually indicates residual secondary tracts, an unidentified internal opening, or active Crohn's disease.
Perianal abscess reformation when the fistula tract becomes obstructed; presents with acute pain, swelling, and fever and requires prompt re-drainage.
Keyhole deformity — an ectropion of the anal canal after fistulotomy that causes mucus leakage and soiling, occasionally requiring revision surgery.

Fournier's gangrene — rare but life-threatening necrotizing fasciitis spreading from perianal sepsis; associated with diabetes, immunosuppression, and delayed presentation, with mortality rates of 15-40%.
Rectovaginal or rectourethral fistula formation as a complication of complex disease or inadvertent surgical injury, requiring specialized reconstructive surgery.

supportResources.name: American Society of Colon and Rectal Surgeons (ASCRS)
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on anal fistula diagnosis and surgical options from the leading US colorectal surgery society.

supportResources.name: Crohn's and Colitis Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient resources on perianal Crohn's fistula, biologic therapy, and support programs.

supportResources.name: NHS — Anal Fistula Patient Information
supportResources.description: Comprehensive UK National Health Service guide covering symptoms, surgery types, and recovery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anal fissure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An anal fissure is a tear in the anal lining causing sharp, knife-like pain during and after defecation with bright-red blood on toilet paper. It does not produce purulent discharge and has no external skin opening. Anal fistula causes chronic discharge from a perianal hole and typically less acute pain during defecation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pilonidal sinus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pilonidal sinus is located in the natal cleft of the buttocks, midline or just off midline, and is caused by embedded hair follicles — not anal gland infection. It discharges through the sacrococcygeal area rather than the perianal skin, has no internal opening in the anal canal, and is managed by different surgical techniques.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hemorrhoids
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hemorrhoids present with painless bright-red bleeding, prolapse, or perianal discomfort but do not produce purulent discharge or create a subcutaneous tract. Prolapsed hemorrhoids may cause mucus leakage but no pus; examination distinguishes them from the granulating external opening of a fistula.

confusedWithConditions.name: Crohn's disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's disease is the underlying systemic condition rather than a differential for fistula itself. However, patients presenting with perianal fistula without prior IBD diagnosis should be screened for Crohn's because up to 25% of fistulas in referral practice are Crohn's-related, requiring biologic therapy in addition to surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Anal abscess (perianal abscess)
relatedConditions.relevance: Perianal abscess is the precursor to anal fistula in over 90% of cryptoglandular cases; the two conditions form a disease continuum of the same cryptoglandular infection process.

relatedConditions.name: Anal fissure
relatedConditions.relevance: Anal fissure and fistula are both anorectal conditions managed by colorectal surgeons; distinguishing between them is a key diagnostic step in patients with perianal pain and discharge.

relatedConditions.name: Crohn's disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Perianal fistula is a defining extra-intestinal manifestation of Crohn's disease and drives major therapeutic decisions including biologic therapy selection and surgical approach.

relatedConditions.name: Ulcerative colitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Ulcerative colitis rarely causes perianal fistulas but is part of the differential IBD evaluation in patients presenting with perianal disease.

relatedConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Rectal carcinoma can rarely present with or cause fistula formation by eroding through the rectal wall; biopsying the tract wall is important in atypical presentations.

faqs.question: What is an anal fistula?
faqs.answer: An anal fistula is a small tunnel connecting the inside of the anal canal to the skin around the anus. It usually develops after a perianal abscess that does not heal properly, leaving a persistent channel through which pus, blood, or stool can discharge. Surgery is needed in most cases because spontaneous healing is rare.

faqs.question: What does anal fistula feel like?
faqs.answer: Most people notice persistent or recurrent discharge of pus or blood near the anus, often staining underwear. There is usually dull perianal ache or throbbing discomfort that temporarily improves when the discharge relieves pressure. Itching, skin irritation, and recurrent swelling at the same spot are also common features.

faqs.question: Can anal fistula heal on its own?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous permanent healing of an anal fistula is rare. The granulation-tissue-lined tract persists and cyclically obstructs, causing repeated abscess formation unless the internal opening is surgically closed. A small minority of Crohn's fistulas enter long-term remission with biologic therapy alone, but surgical drainage is usually still required.

faqs.question: What is the best surgery for anal fistula?
faqs.answer: For simple low fistulas, fistulotomy (laying the tract open) is the most effective procedure with cure rates of 85-95%. For complex fistulas crossing significant sphincter muscle, sphincter-sparing techniques such as the LIFT procedure, mucosal advancement flap, or staged seton are preferred to reduce incontinence risk. Choice depends on tract anatomy assessed by MRI.

faqs.question: How long does anal fistula surgery take to heal?
faqs.answer: Simple fistulotomy wounds heal by secondary intention in four to eight weeks. Larger or complex wounds may take up to twelve weeks. After LIFT or advancement flap repair, most patients resume normal activities within two to four weeks but imaging at three months confirms closure. Crohn's fistulas require months of combined biologic and surgical management.

faqs.question: Is anal fistula related to Crohn's disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Perianal fistulas develop in 15-35% of Crohn's disease patients and are one of its defining extra-intestinal features. Crohn's fistulas tend to be complex, multiple, and resistant to surgery alone. Anti-TNF biologic therapy (infliximab, adalimumab) combined with seton drainage is the standard approach in Crohn's-related fistulas.

faqs.question: Can anal fistula cause incontinence?
faqs.answer: Fistulotomy involving significant external sphincter muscle can cause partial fecal incontinence, particularly in women with prior obstetric sphincter damage or patients with previous anorectal surgery. Modern sphincter-sparing techniques (LIFT, advancement flap) were developed specifically to avoid this risk. Continence assessment before surgery is standard practice.

faqs.question: How is anal fistula diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: examination reveals a perianal opening discharging pus with a palpable subcutaneous tract. MRI pelvis is the gold-standard investigation to map the tract in relation to the sphincter muscles. Examination under anesthesia by a colorectal surgeon is the definitive diagnostic step and is usually combined with the surgical procedure.

faqs.question: What is a seton for anal fistula?
faqs.answer: A seton is a thread (usually silicone or nylon) passed through the fistula tract and tied loosely. A loose (draining) seton controls sepsis and keeps the tract open while the patient undergoes biologic therapy or staging for a definitive repair. A cutting seton is progressively tightened over weeks to slowly divide the muscle, now less commonly used.

faqs.question: What is the LIFT procedure for anal fistula?
faqs.answer: LIFT (ligation of intersphincteric fistula tract) involves identifying the fistula in the intersphincteric groove, tying off the intersphincteric segment, and dividing it without cutting any external sphincter muscle. Cure rates are 60-80% and continence is almost always preserved, making it the preferred first-line sphincter-sparing procedure for transsphincteric fistulas.

faqs.question: Can anal fistula come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence rates are 5-15% after fistulotomy for simple tracts and 20-30% after sphincter-sparing procedures. Recurrence is more likely with Crohn's disease, missed secondary tracts, poorly controlled infection at the time of repair, or inadequate closure of the internal opening. MRI follow-up at three months helps detect early recurrence.

faqs.question: What is a horseshoe fistula?
faqs.answer: A horseshoe fistula is a complex anal fistula in which the tract curves around both sides of the sphincter complex, forming a U-shape that opens on both sides of the perineum. The internal opening is usually in the posterior midline. It represents a challenging surgical problem requiring staged procedures or combined advancement flap with drainage.

faqs.question: Does anal fistula increase cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Long-standing chronic anal fistulas — particularly those associated with Crohn's disease or untreated for many years — carry a small but recognized risk of malignant transformation to mucinous adenocarcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma within the fistula tract. This risk is low in the general population but warrants biopsy of any unusual or thickened fistula wall.

faqs.question: Is anal fistula surgery painful?
faqs.answer: The procedure itself is performed under anesthesia. After fistulotomy, moderate perianal soreness lasting one to two weeks is expected; most patients manage with regular paracetamol and ibuprofen. Sitz baths reduce discomfort. Patients typically return to desk work within one to two weeks and strenuous activity within four to six weeks.

faqs.question: What foods should be avoided with anal fistula?
faqs.answer: Avoid low-fiber foods that cause hard stools and straining, which worsen perianal discomfort and delay wound healing. Reduce alcohol and very spicy foods that increase stool frequency. A high-fiber diet with 25-30 g per day, adequate hydration, and stool softeners where needed are the standard dietary recommendations after anal fistula surgery.

metaTitle: Anal Fistula: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Anal fistula: a tunnel from the anal canal to perianal skin causing pus discharge and recurring abscess. Symptoms, surgery options, and prognosis reviewed.
